does: This paper introduces KARMA, a framework that trains reward models on Reddit conversations to predict response quality based on conversational context and social signals rather than semantic content alone. The framework uses these context-sensitive reward models to fine-tune language models via reinforcement learning, targeting improvement on pragmatics-mediated tasks where tone, norms, and implicit social understanding matter. A key empirical finding is that the highest-performing reward model in predicting Reddit karma does not produce the best downstream aligned models—instead, a simpler model using only conversational context yielded superior alignment outcomes despite lower prediction accuracy on social media metrics.
connects: This work builds on prior research in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) for language model alignment, extending it beyond semantic correctness to pragmatic communication. It responds to limitations in existing reward modeling approaches that focus narrowly on factuality or explicit preferences while ignoring the contextual and social dimensions of effective communication. The paper connects to broader work on learning from social interaction data and implicit reward signals, as well as to research on understanding conversational norms and dialogue coherence. It addresses a known challenge in reward modeling: the misalignment between metrics used to train reward models and actual downstream performance after fine-tuning.
enables: This work opens directions for developing language models that understand and generate socially appropriate behavior beyond factual accuracy. It suggests methods for mining large-scale social interaction datasets to discover implicit human preferences and social conventions. The finding that reward model optimization does not directly translate to better alignment points toward investigating the nature of reward model specification and the gap between performance metrics and genuine model quality. Future work could explore similar context-aware reward modeling for other domains where pragmatic effectiveness depends on implicit social understanding, such as customer service, negotiation, or persuasion. The framework also enables research into mitigating undesirable side effects from reinforcement learning while improving specific behavioral dimensions.
